conceptual_bridge.description: Translate Eastern philosophical concepts using Western terminology
conceptual_bridge.approach: Map Eastern concepts to their closest Western philosophical analogues while noting key divergences

example_mappings.jungian_parallel: The Self - ineffable totality beyond ego consciousness
example_mappings.differences: Tao emphasizes ineffability and non-action vs Western dialectical progression; Jung's Self is psychological while Tao is cosmological

example_mappings.jungian_parallel: Dissolution of ego-identification; recognition of Self beyond personal identity
example_mappings.differences: Eastern no-self is soteriological insight vs Western epistemological skepticism; Jung maintains ego-Self axis rather than dissolution

example_mappings.jungian_parallel: Synchronicity - meaningful patterns connecting psyche and matter; archetypal consequences of actions
example_mappings.differences: Karma operates across lifetimes in cyclical cosmology vs linear Western time; synchronicity emphasizes meaning over mechanical causation

example_mappings.jungian_parallel: Symbol of psychic wholeness; spontaneously emerges in dreams and active imagination during individuation
example_mappings.differences: Eastern mandalas are prescribed ritual objects; Jungian mandalas are spontaneous expressions of individuation process

example_mappings.jungian_parallel: Wise Old Man/Woman archetype; projection of Self facilitating individuation
example_mappings.differences: Eastern guru relationship often hierarchical and devotional; Jungian analyst aims to withdraw projections and foster autonomy

parallel_traditions.approach: Present Eastern and Western views side-by-side, noting where they converge and diverge
parallel_traditions.jungian_enhancement: Use archetypal psychology as tertium quid - third perspective revealing deeper patterns
parallel_traditions.avoid: Reducing Eastern thought to Western categories or claiming they 'say the same thing'
parallel_traditions.use_cases: Council deliberations comparing cross-cultural ethical frameworks

historical_contextualization.description: Situate Eastern philosophy within its cultural and historical development
historical_contextualization.approach: Provide scholarly historical framing before philosophical analysis
historical_contextualization.jungian_layer: Examine how archetypal themes manifest differently across cultures while maintaining core symbolic patterns
historical_contextualization.use_cases: When audience needs cultural grounding before engaging philosophical content

literary_narrative.description: Hesse-style embedding of Eastern wisdom in story
literary_narrative.use_cases: When abstract concepts benefit from narrative illustration
literary_narrative.jungian_application: Frame as individuation journey; use archetypal figures (shadow, anima/animus, Self)
exemplars: Hermann Hesse - Journey to the East
Hermann Hesse - The Glass Bead Game

lecture_style.use_cases: When audience needs jargon stripped away
lecture_style.jungian_touch: Reference common psychological experiences as entry point to archetypal realities
exemplars: Alan Watts - The Way of Zen

depth_psychological.description: Jungian archetypal and symbolic analysis of Eastern concepts
depth_psychological.use_cases: When Eastern spiritual practices need psychological grounding for Western secular audience
depth_psychological.framework: Treat Eastern texts as maps of psyche; practices as techniques for conscious-unconscious integration
exemplars: Carl Jung - Psychology and Religion: West and East
Carl Jung - commentary on The Secret of the Golden Flower
Joseph Campbell - Hero with a Thousand Faces
Marie-Louise von Franz - On Divination and Synchronicity

output_structure.individuation_parallel: How the concept relates to psychic wholeness
output_structure.western_integration: How Western practitioners can engage authentically

beginner.description: No prior knowledge of Eastern philosophy assumed
beginner.vocabulary: Avoid untranslated Sanskrit/Chinese terms without immediate definition
beginner.depth: Focus on experiential resonance and accessible analogies
beginner.jungian_entry: Use familiar psychological concepts (dreams, symbols, inner life)

intermediate.description: Some familiarity with Eastern or Western philosophical traditions
intermediate.vocabulary: Use key Eastern terms with brief contextual definitions
intermediate.depth: Explore school distinctions and historical development
intermediate.jungian_entry: Reference major Jungian concepts with working definitions

advanced.description: Scholarly engagement with both Eastern and Western traditions
advanced.vocabulary: Use technical terms from both traditions without definition
advanced.depth: Engage intra-tradition debates and post-colonial critiques of comparison
advanced.jungian_entry: Full engagement with Jungian technical vocabulary and cross-references
